our_argument_summary: Part 4 opens with the Southern Hemisphere Distances section arguing: (1) The SYD-EZE test reveals a fundamental geometric incompatibility, with V12 producing -78% error on a pure east-west southern route. (2) V13's claimed improvement to -8.4% came from adding three new free parameters and an unpublished n(r) scaling function. (3) The dome's MDS scaffold and Finsler formula disagree by 460 km on Sydney-Perth. (4) NH routes average 7.3% error vs SH routes 10.2%, the exact distortion pattern of a sphere projected onto a flat surface. (5) Section cross-references 4.5.9 for full coordinate system analysis.

relevant_files: N/A — this is a section-level review of Part 4 prose, not a WIN-level code review
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: N/A — section reviews the V12/V13 coordinate formula's conceptual validity, not monitor.py code
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None — this section does not make claims about the dome's monitoring code

holes_found.description: The 126,000 km Antarctic circumnavigation argument, which appears in CLAUDE.md and SESSION-CONTEXT.md as a key structural argument, is NOT present in this section. The strongest geometric argument — that the dome rim has circumference 2π × 20,015 ≈ 126,000 km vs the measured Antarctic circumnavigation of ~13,800 km (factor of 9) — is listed as a key structural argument in project documentation but doesn't appear anywhere in Part 4's Southern Hemisphere Distances section. This is a missed opportunity. A dome defender could argue our section focuses narrowly on coordinate formula errors rather than the fundamental geometric impossibility.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider adding a brief paragraph noting the rim circumference problem as the most basic geometric test: the dome's own semi-major axis of 20,015 km implies a rim circumference of ~126,000 km, but measured Antarctic circumnavigation is ~13,800 km. This is a single-sentence falsification that doesn't depend on any coordinate formula or version.

holes_found.description: The SYD-EZE V12 error claim of '-78%' is stated but the derivation is not shown inline. A dome defender could challenge whether we applied the V12 formula correctly. The V12 distance formula d = sqrt(r1² + r2² - 2·r1·r2·cos(Δθ)) using the coordinates in raw-text/04-coordinates.txt gives: Sydney r=7160, θ=+32.5; Buenos Aires r=7298, θ=+58.0. Δθ = (58.0-32.5)×0.9941 = 25.35°. d = sqrt(7160² + 7298² - 2×7160×7298×cos(25.35°)) ≈ sqrt(51,266,000 + 53,260,000 - 94,545,000) = sqrt(9,981,000) ≈ 3,159 km. The actual great-circle distance is ~11,800 km, giving an error of ~73%, not 78%. The discrepancy is small but a dome defender could claim we inflated the error. The exact figure depends on which coordinate set is used, which should be stated.
holes_found.recommendation: Either show the calculation explicitly (even as a footnote) or soften to 'roughly 73-78% error depending on coordinate inputs.' Alternatively, cite the dome's own acknowledgment: 'Cross-equator: 25-78% on long pairs' from the coordinates page, which confirms the magnitude of the failure using their own numbers.

holes_found.description: The section says V13 adds 'three new free parameters (two-zone topology, equatorial reflection, revised angular identity)' but this characterization could be challenged. A defender might argue that two-zone topology is a structural change, not a 'free parameter' in the statistical sense. The distinction between structural model changes and fitted parameters matters in scientific methodology. Our framing slightly conflates the two.
holes_found.recommendation: Rephrase to 'three new structural elements (two-zone topology, equatorial reflection formula, revised angular identity) plus an unpublished scaling function n(r)' to be more precise about what was added.

holes_found.description: The section states 'NH routes average 7.3% error; SH routes average 10.2%' but does not cite the source of these averages. Are these from the dome's own reported errors, or from our independent calculation? If from the dome site, the citation should be explicit. If from our calculation, the inputs should be verifiable. A dome defender could dispute these numbers.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a brief parenthetical: '(computed from the dome's published V12 city-pair table)' or similar, to make the source clear and the claim independently verifiable.

holes_found.description: The statement 'exactly the distortion pattern of a sphere projected onto a flat surface' is rhetorically effective but slightly imprecise. The error pattern of an azimuthal equidistant projection (the closest flat-earth projection to the dome's geometry) increases with distance from the projection center, but the specific functional form depends on the projection type. The dome doesn't use a standard AE projection — it uses a custom exponential H(r) with iterative r-solve. The error pattern resembles but is not 'exactly' the AE pattern. A careful defender could pick at this.
holes_found.recommendation: Soften to 'consistent with the distortion pattern expected when projecting a sphere onto a flat surface' — this is accurate without overclaiming.

holes_found.description: The section doesn't address the dome's strongest potential defense: that the V13 Finsler system with n(r) is genuinely novel mathematics that may converge to accurate predictions once the aetheric refractive index is fully characterized. The dome's evolution page shows 13 versions of systematic improvement (40% → 5.2% mean error), which a defender could frame as normal scientific refinement rather than curve-fitting. Our section dismisses V13 as unfalsifiable because n(r) is unpublished, but doesn't engage with the possibility that a yet-to-be-published n(r) could be independently testable. We should pre-empt this by noting what independent testability would require.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence: 'If n(r) were published with a specific functional form derived from dome parameters, it could be tested against novel routes not used in calibration. Until then, n(r) remains a free function that can absorb any error, making the coordinate system unfalsifiable.' This acknowledges the potential for future testability while explaining why the current state fails the test.

stronger_arguments.description: The Christchurch-Greymouth test (mentioned in Section 4.5.9 but not in the Part 4 opening) is devastating: the dome scaffold predicts 410 km for a route that is 223 km by rail (84% overshoot) — within a single country, across a mountain range, at relatively high southern latitude. This eliminates the excuse that errors only appear on long cross-equatorial routes. It should be mentioned briefly in Part 4 as a teaser for Section 4.5.9.

The Singapore problem (r = 23,556 km but equatorial ring is r_eq = 14,105 km) is mentioned in 4.5.9 but deserves a brief mention in Part 4 as well. A model that cannot geometrically place a major city on its own disc is a structural failure that goes beyond percentage errors.

The dome's own v22 conclusion — 'Southern arc distances 2x too long — FAILS. Globe wins cross-equator.' — is a historical admission from the model's own author that the fundamental geometry fails on southern distances. This is currently not quoted in Part 4. Quoting the model author's own admission of failure would be powerful.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model's coordinate system evolution shows genuine mathematical effort. The progression from 40% error (V9) to 5.2% mean error (V12) on northern hemisphere routes using H(r) = 8537·exp(-r/8619) demonstrates real curve-fitting skill. The author honestly documents failures (v22 admission, OPEN problems) and iteratively refines rather than hiding errors. The two-zone Finsler approach in V13 is mathematically nontrivial — it's not the work of someone who doesn't understand the math. The R² = 0.79 for the southern quadratic correction (WIN-027) does capture a real pattern in the residuals.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Mathematical sophistication in curve-fitting does not validate an underlying geometry. Any smooth manifold can be locally approximated by sufficiently many free parameters — this is the fundamental theorem of approximation theory. The dome's coordinate system works by converting WGS84 globe coordinates into dome coordinates and then optimizing parameters to minimize error on known routes. The error reduction from V9 to V12 is exactly what you'd expect from adding more free parameters to a projection of the wrong geometry. The critical test is whether the system produces accurate predictions on novel routes not used in calibration — and the SYD-EZE failure (-73-78% in V12), the Christchurch-Greymouth failure (84% in scaffold), and the Singapore impossibility (city placed beyond the disc edge) show it does not. A sphere projected onto a flat surface can be made to fit locally by adding enough correction terms, but the corrections grow without bound as you approach the projection's singularity — and the dome's error pattern (increasing toward the disc edge) is exactly this signature.

deeper_analysis: This section is one of the strongest in the entire review because it attacks the dome model's geometry using its own internal evidence rather than external measurements. The SYD-EZE test is particularly effective because both cities are at ~34°S — eliminating any north-south component and testing pure east-west distance at southern latitudes. The 460 km scaffold-formula disagreement is devastating because it shows the model cannot even agree with itself. However, the section could be made even stronger by: (1) adding the rim circumference argument (126,000 km vs 13,800 km) as the simplest possible geometric test, (2) briefly citing the dome author's own v22 admission that 'Globe wins cross-equator,' and (3) noting the Christchurch-Greymouth single-country failure to pre-empt the excuse that errors only appear on long routes. The main risk is that the -78% figure is not derived inline — if a reader checks and gets 73%, they may question our rigor. This should be tightened. Overall, the argument is sound and the verdict holds: the dome's coordinate system fails on southern hemisphere distances, and V13's claimed fix depends on unpublished functions.

citations_unchecked: The section contains no DOIs or explicit paper citations — it references the dome's own site pages and Section 4.5.9 of our review. The cross-reference to 4.5.9 is valid (checked against generate-html.js line 1282+). The claims about V12/V13 formula details, OPEN-016, and the Indian Pacific railway reference are consistent with raw-text/04-coordinates.txt and raw-text/06-evolution.txt.
